text: The second part of Sir 6:18-37 (vv. 23-31) continues the pericope. While the first part is dedicated to the forming of wisdom, the second part is dedicated to surrendering to wisdom and its consequences. The composition of Sir 6:23-31 is as follows. The first part (vv. 23-25) uses the slavery imagery, the second part (vv. 26-28) uses the hunting imagery, and the third part (vv. 29-31) again uses the slavery imagery. The second part of the composition of the second part of Sir 6:18-37 in the form of a concentric structure with the centre in vv. 6:26-28 shows the development of the thought of the sage. While the first part (vv. 23-25) is dedicated only to surrendering to wisdom, to slavery before wisdom, and to the difficulties that one has to face while searching for wisdom, the third part refers to the first part and describes in allusions the prospects of those who will make an effort to obtain wisdom. The first condition for the obtaining of wisdom is surrender to it, as a slave surrenders to his master. Without this surrender there is no wisdom. The second condition is that the seeking of wisdom should be continuous in all aspects of life. The fruits of the seeking of wisdom are rest, joy and happiness that wisdom will bring to those who obtain it. In the final part of the pericope the sage lists all the possible honours that people can obtain if they will make an effort to obtain wisdom.
